title: New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act
short_title: New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for when factories and power plants need special permits under the Clean Air Act. Under the new rules, a facility only needs a new permit if it actually increases the maximum amount of pollution it can release per hour compared to the past 10 years. Changes made to reduce pollution, improve safety, or make operations more reliable do not require new permits unless the EPA decides they would harm people's health or the environment. The bill also says that changes that do not significantly increase emissions do not count as major modifications that need permits.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 28 - 23.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Clarification of Definition of a Modification: Emission Rate Increases, Pollution Control, Efficiency, Safety, and Reliability Projects
sections.text: This section changes how we define when a factory or power plant has been modified under the Clean Air Act. A change at a facility only counts as increasing pollution if the highest amount of pollution the facility can produce per hour after the change is more than the highest amount it could produce during any hour in the ten years before the change. However, a change does not count as a modification if it is designed to reduce the amount of pollution produced for each unit made, or to make the facility safer or more reliable. The only exception is when the government administrator decides that even these good changes would still cause harm to people's health or the environment because they increase pollution too much.

sections.title: Section 3  Clarification of Definition of Construction for Prevention of Significant Deterioration
sections.text: This section changes the rules for major facilities that produce pollution in areas where air quality is already good. The term construction includes modifications to these facilities, but a modification does not count if it does not cause a significant increase in the actual yearly amount of pollution the facility produces.

sections.title: Section 4  Clarification of Definition of Modifications and Modified for Nonattainment Areas
sections.text: This section changes the rules for major facilities that produce pollution in areas where air quality is already poor. The terms modifications and modified follow the same definition as in Section Two of this law, but they do not include changes that do not cause a significant increase in the actual yearly amount of pollution the facility produces.

sections.title: Section 5  Rule of Construction
sections.text: Nothing in this law should be understood to make any change count as a modification under the Clean Air Act if that same change would not have been considered a modification before this law was passed.
